questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of COPIOUS:
questions.solution: Abundant

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: modern / has / technology / efficiency / improved / significantly
options: Modern technology has improved efficiency significantly
Efficiency has modern technology improved significantly
Modern technology has improved efficiency significantly
Improved efficiency has significantly modern technology
Technology modern has efficiency improved significantly
questions.solution: Modern technology has improved efficiency significantly

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of RIGOROUS:
questions.solution: Thorough

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: company / its / announced / expansion / has / plans / the
options: The company has announced its expansion plans
The company has announced its expansion plans
Has the company announced its expansion plans
Expansion plans the company has announced
Announced the company its expansion plans has
questions.solution: The company has announced its expansion plans

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of JUBILANT:
questions.solution: Joyful

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: corruption / development / hinders / economic
questions.solution: Corruption hinders economic development

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of EPHEMERAL:
questions.solution: Transient

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: responsible / are / for / who / safety / miners / ensuring
options: Responsible for miners safety who is
Miners safety who is responsible for ensuring
Who is responsible for ensuring miners safety
Who is responsible for ensuring miners safety
Ensuring safety for miners who is responsible
questions.solution: Who is responsible for ensuring miners safety

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of OSTENTATIOUS:
questions.solution: Showy

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: research / this / significant / has / implications / study / for
options: This study has significant implications for research
This study has significant implications for research
Significant implications this study has for research
Research this study has significant implications for
For research this study has significant implications
questions.solution: This study has significant implications for research

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of AUDACIOUS:
questions.solution: Bold

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: environmental / concerns / need / planning / careful
questions.solution: Environmental concerns need careful planning

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PERIPHERAL:
questions.solution: Outer

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: renewable / future / energy / is / the
questions.solution: Renewable energy is the future

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of CANDID:
questions.solution: Frank

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: country / infrastructure / of / development / backbone / the / is
options: The backbone of country development is infrastructure
Development of country backbone is infrastructure
Country development backbone is the infrastructure
Infrastructure is the backbone of country development
Infrastructure is the backbone of country development
questions.solution: Infrastructure is the backbone of country development

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of ADMONISH:
questions.solution: Reprimand

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: The company (A) / is planning to (B) / set up (C) / a new industry. (D)
questions.solution: No error

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To be in the same boat':
options: To be in the same situation
questions.solution: To be in the same situation

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of PRAGMATIC:
questions.solution: Idealistic

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of FACILITATE:
questions.solution: Simplify

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: Each of the participants were given a certificate.
options: Every of the participants were given a certificate.
Each of the participants were given a certificate.
Each of the participants was given a certificate.
Each of the participant was given a certificate.
questions.solution: Each of the participants was given a certificate.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of ABSTAIN:
questions.solution: Refrain

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She not only lost her wallet but also her phone.
options: She lost not only her wallet but also.
She lost not only her wallet but also her phone.
She not only lost her wallet but also lost.
She not only lost her wallet but also her phone.
questions.solution: She lost not only her wallet but also her phone.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of LETHARGIC:
questions.solution: Sluggish

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of mergesort for n=128 is:
questions.solution: Mergesort always O(n log n). For n=128: O(128 log 128).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ON DELETE CASCADE means:
options: Deleting a parent row also deletes all child rows referencing it
questions.solution: CASCADE: propagate. SET NULL: set FK to NULL. RESTRICT: prevent delete. NO ACTION: defer check.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A shift register can be used as:
options: Serial-to-parallel converter, delay line, or counter
questions.solution: Shift register: serial in/out, parallel in/out. SIPO, PISO, SISO, PIPO modes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Accept action in LR parsing occurs:
options: When the start symbol is on top and input is empty (lookahead = $)
questions.solution: Accept: parser successfully recognizes the input (state containing [S' → S·, $]).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
Speeds up both reads and writes equally
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The symbol table in a compiler is often implemented as:
options: A hash table (or tree) for fast lookup
questions.solution: Symbol table: hash table maps name → attributes (type, scope, offset). Scope management via stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting n elements into a dynamic array that doubles on resize has an amortized insertion cost of:
questions.solution: Doubling makes total work O(n) over n inserts, i.e. amortized O(1) each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SQL stands for:
questions.solution: SQL = standard language for relational databases.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Heap memory allocation strategies:
questions.solution: First-fit: quick. Best-fit: minimal waste but slow. Worst-fit: leaves larger fragments.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Kruskal's algorithm builds MST by:
options: Sorting edges by weight and adding if they don't create a cycle (union-find)
questions.solution: Kruskal: O(E log E + E α(V)) with union-find. Globally sorts edges. Good for sparse graphs.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The two-phase commit (2PC) is used for:
questions.solution: 2PC: prepare phase (vote) + commit phase (decision). Ensures atomicity across nodes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An essential prime implicant is:
options: A prime implicant that covers a minterm not covered by any other
questions.solution: Essential PI = must be included in minimal SOP (covers a unique minterm).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The liveness of a variable is:
options: The set of points where the variable holds a needed value
questions.solution: Live range: from definition to last use. Dead variables can have registers reused.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The extended Euclidean algorithm computes:
options: GCD(a,b) and coefficients x,y such that ax + by = GCD(a,b)
questions.solution: Extended Euclid: returns (gcd, x, y) where ax + by = gcd(a,b). Used in RSA.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A LEFT JOIN returns:
options: All rows from left table and matching rows from right (NULL for no match)
questions.solution: LEFT JOIN: preserves left table rows. RIGHT JOIN: preserves right rows.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Karnaugh map with 4 variables has:
questions.solution: 4 variables = 2^4 = 16 cells in K-map.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dynamic scoping means:
options: Scope is determined at compile time
Variable scope is determined by the call chain at runtime
questions.solution: Dynamic scoping: a function uses the most recent binding from the call stack. Used in Lisp (early dialects).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum bipartite matching can be found using:
questions.solution: Bipartite matching = network flow (source → left → right → sink, capacity 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Multiversion concurrency control (MVCC):
options: Maintains multiple versions of data items; readers see consistent snapshot
questions.solution: MVCC: writers create new version, readers see older version (no writer blocks reader).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OR gate outputs 1 when:
questions.solution: OR = 1 if any input = 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Type checking in compilers:
options: Verifies that operations have operands of compatible types
questions.solution: Type checking: static (compile-time) or dynamic (runtime). Strong vs weak typing.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The minimum spanning tree of a graph has:
questions.solution: MST = tree connecting all vertices with n-1 edges.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: READ COMMITTED prevents:
questions.solution: READ COMMITTED (default in PostgreSQL, Oracle): cannot read uncommitted changes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=0</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=1</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">NOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>
figure.caption: NOR gate

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A parser generator like YACC produces:
options: An LALR(1) parser from a grammar specification
questions.solution: YACC/Bison = LALR(1) parser generator. Lex/Flex = lexer generator.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The traveling salesman problem (TSP) is:
questions.solution: TSP decision (is there a tour ≤ k?) is NP-complete. Optimization is NP-hard.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The log sequence number (LSN):
options: Uniquely identifies a log record (monotonically increasing, stored in page headers)
questions.solution: LSN in page header: pageLSN (last log record affecting page), recLSN (oldest recovery LSN).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The modulus of a counter is:
options: The number of states the counter cycles through
questions.solution: Mod-n counter: n states (e.g., decade counter = mod-10, 0-9).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Intermediate code generation uses:
questions.solution: TAC: x = y op z (three-address). Quadruples: (op, arg1, arg2, result).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of nodes in a complete binary tree of height h=10 (root at 0) is at most:
questions.solution: Max nodes in complete binary tree of height h = 2^(h+1)-1 = 2047.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL LIMIT clause:
options: Restricts the number of rows returned
questions.solution: LIMIT (MySQL, PostgreSQL) or TOP (SQL Server) or ROWNUM (Oracle).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A counter that counts 0-7 is a:
questions.solution: 3 bits = 8 states (0 to 7). Mod-8 counter.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A basic block in compiler optimization:
options: A sequence of straight-line code with one entry and one exit
questions.solution: Basic block = maximal straight-line code sequence (no jumps in, no jumps out except last).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Chinese Remainder Theorem is used for:
questions.solution: CRT: x ≡ a₁ (mod n₁), x ≡ a₂ (mod n₂) → unique solution mod N = n₁·n₂.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The PLA (Programmable Logic Array) has:
options: AND plane followed by OR plane (both programmable)
questions.solution: PLA: programmable AND array + programmable OR array. PAL: fixed OR, programmable AND.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Constant folding replaces:
options: Constant expressions with their computed values
questions.solution: Constant folding: 3 + 5 → 8 (compile-time evaluation).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Bellman-Ford algorithm can detect:
questions.solution: Bellman-Ford detects negative cycles (iterates V-1 times + 1 check).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization is used to:
options: Reduce data redundancy and eliminate anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization = organizing data to minimize redundancy (1NF, 2NF, 3NF, BCNF).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: NOR gate is:
questions.solution: NOR = NOT(OR). Also universal gate.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Error recovery in parsing:
options: Attempts to continue parsing after a syntax error by skipping tokens
questions.solution: Error recovery: panic mode (skip until sync token), phrase-level, error productions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The KMP algorithm for string matching uses:
options: Prefix function (failure function) to avoid backtracking
questions.solution: KMP: prefix function computes longest proper prefix that is also suffix.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A transaction is a:
options: Logical unit of work (one or more SQL operations)
questions.solution: Transaction = group of operations as a single logical unit.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Attributed grammar associates:
questions.solution: Attribute grammar = CFG + semantic attributes + rules (for type checking, code gen).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(5) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 8, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Granularity of locks (hierarchy):
options: Database → Table → Page → Row → Attribute (increasing concurrency)
questions.solution: Lock granularity trade-off: finer = more concurrency + more overhead. Intent locks in hierarchical locking.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is correct?
options: Any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates
questions.solution: NAND (and NOR) are the universal gates; AND/OR/XOR alone are not functionally complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Intermediate code generation produces:
questions.solution: Intermediate representation (IR): TAC, quadruples, SSA form.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(7) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 21, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: ACID stands for:
questions.solution: ACID = properties of database transactions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the XOR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 110" width="220" height="110"><line x1="10" y1="35" x2="70" y2="35" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="10" y1="75" x2="70" y2="75" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="6" y="30" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">A=1</text><text x="6" y="92" font-size="11" fill="#2563eb">B=0</text><path d="M70 20 L110 20 Q150 55 110 90 L70 90 Z" fill="#f1f5f9" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="95" y="60" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">XOR</text><line x1="150" y1="55" x2="200" y2="55" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="2"/><text x="180" y="48" font-size="11" fill="#dc2626">?</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer analysis determines:
options: Which memory locations a pointer can refer to
questions.solution: Pointer analysis (alias analysis) = which variables a pointer may point to (for optimization).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The solution of T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 is:
questions.solution: Binary search recurrence: T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 = O(log n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Replication in databases:
options: Copies data to multiple servers for availability and read scaling
questions.solution: Replication: master-slave (async/sync), multi-master, or peer-to-peer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Velocity in Scrum is:
options: The amount of work a team completes in a sprint (measured in story points)
questions.solution: Velocity: past sprints' story points completed. Used for capacity planning (not productivity).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The knapsack problem (0/1) is:
questions.solution: 0/1 knapsack: NP-complete (decision). Optimization is NP-hard. Pseudo-polynomial via DP (O(nW)).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Post Correspondence Problem (PCP) is:
questions.solution: PCP: undecidable (can encode TM computations).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: IPv6 header is simpler because:
options: No checksum, no fragmentation by routers, no options (extension headers)
questions.solution: IPv6 streamlined: no IP-level checksum, no hop-by-hop fragmentation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The time to build a heap of n elements is:
questions.solution: Build-heap: O(n) using Floyd's method (sift-down from n/2 to 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Excess-3 for decimal 5 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The relational model was proposed by:
questions.solution: Codd's 1970 paper 'A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks'.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 127 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Treap combines:
questions.solution: Treap = tree + heap. Each node has key (BST) and priority (heap). Random priority ≈ balanced.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The interrupt handler is also known as:
questions.solution: ISR: handles hardware interrupts (save registers, service device, restore).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Maximum flow (Ford-Fulkerson):
options: Repeatedly finds augmenting paths (residual graph) until no more
questions.solution: Ford-Fulkerson: O(E × max_flow) with DFS. Edmonds-Karp (BFS): O(VE²). Dinic: O(V²E) for unit capacities.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CPU consists of:
questions.solution: CPU = ALU + Control + Registers + Cache.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum Ethernet frame payload (MTU) is:
questions.solution: Ethernet II: destination(6)+source(6)+type(2)+payload(46-1500)+FCS(4) = 64-1518 bytes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hub operates at which OSI layer?
questions.solution: Hub = Layer 1 (repeats signals). Switch = Layer 2 (forwards frames). Router = Layer 3 (routes packets).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The term 'phishing' refers to:
options: Fraudulent attempt to obtain sensitive information by disguising as a trustworthy entity
questions.solution: Phishing = social engineering via email/website impersonation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Selective ACK (SACK) allows:
questions.solution: SACK: receiver can inform sender about individual received segments (better loss recovery).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: CIDR notation /24 represents which subnet mask?
questions.solution: /24 = 24 bits for network = 255.255.255.0.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 2 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?
questions.solution: Subnets = 2^2 = 4; host bits = 6 ⇒ 2^6 − 2 = 62 hosts each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A bridge operates at:
questions.solution: Bridge: connects two LAN segments, forwards frames based on MAC address. Modern bridges = switches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linear search of an unsorted array of n items takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: It may examine every element, so worst-case time is O(n).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 10 ns, memory access = 200 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.9 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: B-trees are commonly used in:
questions.solution: B-tree: optimized for disk I/O (each node = one disk block). Used in ext4, NTFS, DB indexes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Rabin-Karp string matching uses:
options: Rolling hash (hash of pattern compared to hash of each substring)
questions.solution: Rabin-Karp: average O(n+m), worst O(nm) (many hash collisions). Rolling hash: O(1) substring hash update.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 8, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Perfect forwarding in C++:
options: Preserves value category (lvalue/rvalue) when forwarding arguments to another function
questions.solution: Perfect forwarding: template<typename T> void f(T&& t) { g(std::forward<T>(t)); }. Reference collapsing rules.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Branch prediction is used to:
questions.solution: Branch prediction guesses branch direction to avoid stalling.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 20, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Type-0 grammar corresponds to:
questions.solution: Type 0 = unrestricted grammar = TM (most powerful).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A buffer overflow occurs when:
options: A file is opened too many times
Data written exceeds the allocated buffer size
questions.solution: Buffer overflow: writing past array bounds (security vulnerability).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Linux uses which type of kernel?
questions.solution: Linux: monolithic + loadable kernel modules (LKM) for dynamic loading.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A monitor is a:
options: High-level synchronization construct with condition variables
questions.solution: Monitor = language-level construct for mutual exclusion + condition variables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging eliminates:
questions.solution: Paging eliminates external fragmentation. May have internal (last page).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pairing heap:
options: A simple and efficient self-adjusting heap (merge in O(1), delete-min in O(log n) amortized)
questions.solution: Pairing heap: simplifies Fibonacci heap. Good practical performance (used in some C++ STL implementations).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Slow start doubles the congestion window:
questions.solution: Slow start: cwnd ← cwnd × 2 per RTT (or +1 per ACK). Until ssthresh.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A firewall can:
options: Filter packets based on IP, port, protocol (stateful or stateless)
questions.solution: Firewall: packet filter, stateful inspection, application-layer (proxy), next-gen.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Boyer-Moore uses:
options: Bad character rule and good suffix rule (worst-case O(nm) but sublinear in practice)
questions.solution: Boyer-Moore: compares right-to-left. Bad character: shift to align mismatch with last occurrence. Good suffix: match suffix.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 1's complement of 10110 is:
questions.solution: 1's complement: flip all bits. 10110 → 01001.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a necessary condition for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock requires NO preemption; allowing preemption actually prevents deadlock.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Post Correspondence Problem (PCP):
options: Is undecidable (determine if sequence of dominos matches)
questions.solution: PCP: given dominoes (x_i, y_i), find sequence where concatenated x = concatenated y. Undecidable.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 14, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /27 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?
questions.solution: Host bits = 32 − 27 = 5; usable = 2^5 − 2 = 30.
